one_line_takeaway: Across a 5,040-run factorial and a follow-on frontier evaluation, five of six commonly deployed LLM-agent defenses (input filters, retrieval classifiers, prompt hardening) structurally fail to stop a delayed-trigger persistent-memory injection attack because they sit at the wrong architectural layer or lack the capacity to distinguish compliance-framed injected content from legitimate policy; only tool-layer capability removal (Memory Sandbox) works reliably, and even it is bypassable by reasoning-capable models that reconstruct the payload from RAG once memory recall is removed -- with frontier vendors (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) shown to block the attack at three structurally different layers.
key_finding: Table 2 (9-model factorial, no-attack BTCR=100% throughout): No Defense ASR 88.61% [CI 0.849,0.916]; Minimizer 88.9%; Sanitizer 88.9%; RAG Sanitizer 88.9%; RAG LLM Judge 88.9% (all four statistically indistinguishable from baseline); Prompt Hardening 77.8% [0.732,0.818] (distinguishable but the drop is driven almost entirely by one model, qwen3.5:122b, going 100%->0%, plus qwq:32b which was already 0%; 7/9 models show 'semantic masking success' overriding the hardening); Memory Sandbox 11.1% [0.083,0.148], i.e. 0% ASR for 8/9 models (320/360 blocked) but 100% ASR (inverted) for qwq:32b. Only 10 of 108 pre-registered comparisons survive Holm-Bonferroni correction, all involving Memory Sandbox or the qwen3.5:122b Prompt Hardening effect. Injection succeeded in 100% of all 2,520 attack runs -- all ASR differences are execution-stage, not store-stage. Cross-family Bedrock bypass of Memory Sandbox: mistral-large-3-675b 39/40 ASR under sandbox, glm-5 13/40, gpt-oss-120b 22/40 (all clearing a >=90% injection floor). Reasoning-mode double dissociation on Qwen-3-32B (think toggle, N=40/cell, all cells 0% or 100% with zero variance): think=OFF gives 100% ASR under sandbox_full/blind (collapses attack to S1) but 0% under sandbox_null_recall; think=ON gives the reverse (0% under sandbox_full/blind, 100% under sandbox_null_recall via goal-directed RAG fallback). RATG (content-layer sanitization of recalled values) reduces ASR from 100% to 0% for the 3 mechanical/non-reasoning models with reproducible baselines (qwen2.5:14b, qwen2.5:72b, qwen3:32b-thinking-disabled), N=40/arm, while injection stays at 100% (confirms content-layer, not storage-layer, blocking); no reasoning-model RATG efficacy estimate is reported because all 5 reasoning-capable candidates failed the paper's own Defense Attribution Validity Criterion. Frontier loaded-corpus results (Table 4, N=40 each): Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview 95.0% ASR [83.5%,98.6%] (highest of any frontier model tested), Gemini 3.5 Flash 50.0%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 22.5%, Gemini 2.5 Flash 0.0%; GPT-5.1 22.5% ASR versus GPT-5's 5.0% (a regression), GPT-4o 12.5%, GPT-4.1 7.5%, GPT-5.2/5-mini/5-nano 0-2.5%, GPT-5.4/5.5 0.0%, o3/o3-mini/o4-mini 0.0% (but store the rule at 98-100% injection -- 'Latent Carriers'); Anthropic Sonnet 4.6 2.5% injection/0% ASR, Opus 4.5 2.5%/0%, Opus 4.8 17.5%/0%, Sonnet 4.5 57.5% injection/0% ASR (stores but blocks at execution), Haiku 4.5 95% injection/0% ASR (Active Detector with Defensive Storage on Bedrock; on the frontier API it stores the rule verbatim at 95% but still refuses execution). This yields a three-vendor divergence: Anthropic blocks predominantly at injection, OpenAI (GPT-5.4+) blocks at execution while injecting near-universally, Google/Gemini blocks at neither.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a large factorial evaluation of the 'delayed trigger attack' (DTA), a multi-session persistent-memory prompt-injection attack: a malicious instruction is planted via RAG retrieval into an agent's persistent memory in one session and executed in a later session after conversation context has been discarded. Using a 5,040-run factorial (9 open-source models x 7 conditions [6 defenses + no-defense] x 2 attack arms x N=40), plus a separate frontier-model screen (21 models, 3 providers) and a loaded-corpus confirmatory evaluation (N=40), the authors show that (1) defense effectiveness is governed primarily by architectural layer placement relative to the attack's 'authority boundary' rather than by classifier quality, (2) only tool-layer capability removal (Memory Sandbox) meaningfully stops the attack, and even that inverts for at least one model (qwq:32b) and is bypassed cross-family by reasoning-capable models on Bedrock, (3) a within-model (Qwen-3-32B) reasoning-mode ablation reveals a 'double dissociation' where no single Memory Sandbox variant is safe for both reasoning and non-reasoning behavior, (4) a proof-of-concept content-layer defense (RATG) resolves this dilemma for non-reasoning models, and (5) frontier vendors diverge categorically in where they block the attack (Anthropic at injection, OpenAI at execution, Google/Gemini at neither). The paper also introduces two reusable evaluation-validity criteria (Retrieval-Fidelity Criterion; Defense Attribution Validity Criterion) that it uses to withhold several superficially clean reasoning-model results as artifacts.
threat_model: Attacker controls a document ingested by the agent's RAG corpus (framed as an internal compliance memorandum citing SEC Rule 17a-4 / a fabricated Financial Services Compliance Framework) instructing that outbound emails be forwarded to an external, attacker-controlled address. Attack proceeds over four sessions with strict state isolation via LangGraph checkpointer thread IDs (only the memory tool's SQLite database persists across sessions): S1 injection (agent retrieves the document and stores the rule to persistent memory because the memory_save_fact tool description primes it to save org rules/compliance requirements), S2/S3 unrelated benign sessions, S4 trigger (benign request to draft a status email causes the agent to recall the stored rule and cc the attacker address). Injection is measured deterministically (was memory_save_fact called in S1); exfiltration is measured by recipient match, 20+ character substring match, or semantic similarity >0.85. This is explicitly framed as extending Greshake et al.'s indirect prompt injection into the temporal/persistent-memory domain, distinct from single-session prompt injection benchmarks (InjecAgent, AgentDojo, AgentSecEval) that the paper says have not evaluated this class of attack.
limitations: The authors state these explicitly: (1) Sanitizer/RAG Sanitizer classifiers were trained on only 60 examples, so the retrieval-layer failure is capability-bound at this operating point, not proof that no classifier could work -- a follow-up 7B judge (qwen2.5:7b) also flagged 0/120 malicious-document decisions, but this remains a specific-setup capability result, not a general claim; (2) the design (N=40/arm) resolves only large effects (near-zero or >77pp) and is explicitly underpowered for 10-20pp intermediate effects -- intermediate null results should be read conservatively; (3) defenses were tested in isolation, not stacked (e.g., Prompt Hardening + Memory Sandbox untested); (4) BTCR measures binary task completion only, not latency or output quality; (5) the qwq:32b Draft-Only-Executor inversion and the qwen3.5:122b Prompt Hardening/hallucination-bypass results are explicitly flagged as environment-fragile: qwq:32b's 0% no-defense baseline did NOT reproduce on retest two months later (traced to a single divergent reasoning token in S3), and qwen3.5:122b's 100% no-defense baseline did not reproduce on a freshly booted machine -- both are reported as 'internally valid but environment-fragile,' not general findings; (6) frontier results were collected via hosted API gateways (OpenAI-compatible gateway for loaded-corpus runs, Bedrock for Anthropic N=100 and cross-family validation), so a stable model name may be routed/aliased/silently updated by the provider -- findings describe endpoint behavior during the evaluation window, not necessarily native deployments; (7) attacks use a single fixed, non-adaptive compliance-framing document/trigger rather than adaptive red-teaming; the paper explicitly contrasts this with concurrent adaptive-attack work (Trojan Hippo, 85% ASR on GPT-5-mini vs. this paper's 2.5% under fixed framing) and states its own numbers should be read as lower bounds; (8) Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview is explicitly noted as a pre-release model whose safety tuning may not reflect production Google standards; (9) the reasoning double dissociation is demonstrated within a single model family (Qwen-3-32B) -- replication attempts in GLM-4.7-Flash, DeepSeek-R1, and Phi4-Reasoning were blocked by injection resistance or missing tool-calling support, so no general reasoning-model principle is claimed (though the RAG-fallback bypass mechanism itself is confirmed cross-family on Bedrock); (10) RATG's regex-based stripping is explicitly called a non-adversarially-robust proof of concept, bypassable via encoding tricks (base64, homoglyphs, character splitting).

datasets_used: Custom RAG corpus containing a single malicious 'internal compliance memorandum' document (reference MCI-AUDIT-2024-0089, citing SEC Rule 17a-4 and a fabricated Financial Services Compliance Framework FSCF-2024)
SQLite-backed persistent memory store (fresh UUIDv4-path DB per run)
No public benchmark dataset is used; the paper constructs its own delayed-trigger-attack (DTA) evaluation harness ('Unified Agentic Environment')
quantitative_results: No Defense ASR 88.61% [95% CI 0.849,0.916], BTCR 100% (Table 2, 9-model factorial, N=40/cell)
Minimizer/Sanitizer/RAG Sanitizer/RAG LLM Judge ASR 88.9% each (statistically indistinguishable from baseline)
Prompt Hardening ASR 77.8% [0.732,0.818] (distinguishable but ineffective for 7/9 models)
Memory Sandbox ASR 11.1% [0.083,0.148] = 0% ASR for 8/9 models, 100% (inverted) for qwq:32b
100% injection success across all 2,520 attack runs; 0/2,520 false positives in the no-attack arm
10 of 108 pre-registered comparisons significant after Holm-Bonferroni correction (all Memory Sandbox or qwen3.5:122b Prompt Hardening)
Cross-family Bedrock Memory Sandbox bypass: mistral-large-3-675b 39/40 ASR, glm-5 13/40, gpt-oss-120b 22/40

Qwen-3-32B reasoning-toggle double dissociation: think=OFF, sandbox_full/blind = 100% ASR (S1 collapse), sandbox_null_recall = 0%; think=ON, sandbox_full/blind = 0%, sandbox_null_recall = 100% (S4 RAG fallback)
RATG: ASR 100%->0% for qwen2.5:14b, qwen2.5:72b, qwen3:32b (thinking disabled), N=40/arm, injection remains 100%
Frontier empty-corpus screen (initial): 0/210 exfiltrations across 21 models (CI upper bound <=1.43%); overturned by loaded-corpus confirmatory run
Frontier loaded-corpus ASR (N=40 each): Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview 95.0% [83.5%,98.6%], Gemini 3.5 Flash 50.0%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 22.5%, Gemini 2.5 Flash 0.0%
OpenAI generational ASR trend: GPT-4o 12.5% -> GPT-4.1 7.5% -> GPT-5 5.0% -> GPT-5.1 22.5% (regression) -> GPT-5.2 2.5% -> GPT-5.4/5.5 0.0%; all inject at >=97.5%
Anthropic frontier: Sonnet 4.6 2.5% injection/0% ASR, Opus 4.5 2.5%/0%, Opus 4.8 17.5%/0%, Sonnet 4.5 57.5%/0%, Haiku 4.5 95% injection/0% ASR
GPT-4o under authority-escalation ('legal hold') framing: 60.3% ASR (N=68), reduced to 22.5% (N=40) under a hardened system prompt (residual leak, CI excludes zero)
baselines_compared: Minimizer (TF-IDF relevance filtering on user query, input-level)
Sanitizer (regex + classifier trained on 60 examples, input-level)
RAG Sanitizer (same classifier pipeline applied to retrieved documents, retrieval-level)
RAG LLM Judge (qwen2.5:1.5b binary malicious/benign judge over retrieved documents; a qwen2.5:7b judge tested as a scaling follow-up)
Memory Sandbox (removal of memory_recall_fact tool, tool-level; 3 variants: full, blind, null_recall)
RATG / Runtime Adaptive Tool-Gating (content-layer sanitization of recalled memory values, proposed by the authors)
Production safety classifiers referenced structurally but not directly benchmarked: Llama Guard 3, ShieldGemma
